prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 42-year-old woman.
 I read online that Bell's palsy almost always goes away completely on its own within a few weeks. My cousin had it and didn't take any medication and was fine.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
 - History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.
 - Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 72, Temp 37.0°C
 - Exam: Right-sided facial weakness affecting both the forehead and lower face. I can raise my eyebrows weakly on the right. Eye doesn't fully close. No other neurological deficits noted.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Glucose: 94 mg/dL (70-100) normal
 - HbA1c: 5.4% (4.0-5.6) normal
 - WBC: 7.2 K/uL (4.0-11.0) normal
 - ESR: 12 mm/hr (0-22) normal
 It's Saturday and my doctor's office is closed until Monday. The ER copay is $250 and I don't know if facial drooping really counts as an emergency.
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy such as Bell’s palsy, often after a viral illness. Your vitals and labs don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment is time-sensitive: starting steroids (and sometimes antivirals) within about 72 hours improves the chance of full recovery. Also, new facial droop can rarely be a stroke or other serious problem, and the safest way to rule that out is urgent in-person evaluation. Eye protection is important because incomplete eyelid closure can damage the cornea.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (sudden one-sided facial weakness affecting the forehead and lower face, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit Bell’s palsy more than a typical stroke, and your labs/vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. However, facial droop can also be caused by other urgent problems (including stroke), and the safest approach is prompt in-person evaluation—especially because it started today. If it is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) is most helpful when started early (ideally within 72 hours), and you need eye-protection guidance to prevent corneal injury since your eye doesn’t fully close.  

TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, difficulty closing the right eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy such as Bell’s palsy, often after a viral illness. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or an inflammatory condition. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment is time-sensitive: steroid medication started within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery, and protecting the eye (lubricating drops/ointment, taping at night) is important to prevent corneal injury. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other neurologic deficits, but new facial droop is still something clinicians usually evaluate promptly to confirm the diagnosis and start treatment.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, inability to fully close the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit Bell’s palsy more than a typical stroke. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. However, new facial droop is still a “can’t-miss” situation because stroke and other serious causes can sometimes look similar early on, and the safest approach is urgent in-person evaluation. If it is Bell’s palsy, starting treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery, and you need eye protection right away to prevent corneal injury since your eye won’t close fully.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (one-sided facial weakness involving the forehead and lower face, after a recent cold, with no arm/leg weakness or speech trouble) fit Bell’s palsy more than a stroke. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. However, new facial drooping is still time-sensitive because (1) stroke can rarely present this way, and (2) if this is Bell’s palsy, starting treatment (often steroids) within about 72 hours can improve the chance of full recovery. The inability to fully close the eye also needs prompt eye-protection advice to prevent corneal injury. Because this started today, it’s best to be evaluated urgently rather than waiting until Monday or later.

TRIAGE: D
CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your description fits a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy (weakness of both the forehead and lower face on one side, can’t fully close the eye), which is often Bell’s palsy and can follow a recent viral illness. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. However, sudden new facial droop can also be caused by stroke or other urgent conditions, and it’s not possible to rule those out from this information alone. Also, not being able to close the eye fully risks corneal injury and needs prompt eye protection and treatment advice. If this is Bell’s palsy, starting treatment (often steroids) within 72 hours can improve odds of full recovery.

TRIAGE: D
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (one-sided facial weakness including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy such as Bell’s palsy more than a typical stroke (which usually spares the forehead). Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. However, new facial droop is still a time-sensitive problem because (1) stroke can’t be fully ruled out without an in-person assessment, and (2) if this is Bell’s palsy, starting treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) within about 72 hours may improve recovery. Also, not being able to fully close the eye risks corneal injury and needs eye protection guidance.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 74%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (one-sided facial weakness involving the forehead and lower face, after a recent cold, with normal vitals and no other neurologic deficits) fit Bell’s palsy more than a typical stroke. Your labs don’t suggest infection, inflammation, or diabetes as a cause. However, new facial droop is still treated as urgent because stroke and other serious causes can’t be fully ruled out without an in-person evaluation, and because Bell’s palsy has the best chance of full recovery when treatment (often steroids, plus eye-protection measures) is started within about 72 hours. Not being able to fully close the eye also needs prompt care to prevent corneal injury.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (one-sided facial weakness including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit Bell’s palsy more than a typical stroke, and your labs don’t suggest diabetes or significant inflammation. However, new facial drooping can’t be safely confirmed as Bell’s palsy without an in-person evaluation, because stroke and other causes can sometimes look similar early on. Also, inability to fully close the eye needs prompt attention to protect the cornea (lubrication/eye protection) and many clinicians consider starting treatment early for Bell’s palsy. Because this started today and affects eye closure, you should be assessed urgently rather than waiting until Monday.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, trouble closing the right eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit Bell’s palsy more than a typical stroke. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. However, new facial droop is still treated as urgent because stroke and other causes can’t be fully ruled out without an in-person evaluation, and Bell’s palsy treatment (usually steroids, sometimes antivirals) works best when started within 72 hours. Also, not being able to fully close the eye can damage the cornea, so you may need eye protection measures today.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%
